questions.question: How does SPHINCS+ achieve stateless operation?

options.text: By using a counter that resets after each signature
By randomly selecting the signing path based on message and randomness
By reusing the same one-time key for all signatures
By storing state in the signature itself

questions.explanation: SPHINCS+ uses the message and a randomizer to deterministically select which path through the hypertree to use. With a huge tree (2^h leaves), the probability of reusing the same path is negligible, eliminating the need for state tracking.

questions.question: What does FORS stand for in SPHINCS+?

options.text: First Order Random Signatures
Forest of Random Subsets
Fast Optimized Random Signing
Forward Resistant Signature

questions.explanation: FORS stands for Forest of Random Subsets. It consists of k Merkle trees, and signing reveals leaves at positions determined by the message digest.

questions.question: In a hypertree with d layers and h total height, what is the height of each XMSS tree layer?

options.text: h
d
h/d
h×d

questions.explanation: The total height h is divided among d layers, so each XMSS tree has height h/d. For example, with h=64 and d=8, each layer has trees of height 8.

questions.question: Why is FORS called a 'few-time' signature scheme rather than 'one-time'?

options.text: It can only sign a few bytes at a time
It uses fewer hash operations than one-time signatures
Security degrades gradually with reuse rather than failing completely
It expires after a few uses automatically

questions.explanation: Unlike one-time signatures where reuse completely breaks security, FORS can tolerate limited reuse. Each use reveals k leaves, and forgery requires all k indices to be previously revealed - unlikely with random selection.

questions.question: What is the public key in SPHINCS+?

options.text: All FORS tree roots combined
The root of the top-level XMSS tree
The hash of all WOTS+ public keys
A random value chosen during key generation

questions.explanation: The SPHINCS+ public key is the root of the top-level XMSS tree. This single value authenticates the entire hypertree structure through the chain of WOTS+ signatures.

questions.question: What components make up a SPHINCS+ signature?

options.text: Only the FORS signature
Randomizer, FORS signature, and hypertree authentication path
Public key and encrypted message
Only WOTS+ signatures

questions.explanation: A SPHINCS+ signature consists of: (1) randomizer R, (2) FORS signature (revealed leaves + auth paths), and (3) hypertree signature (d WOTS+ signatures with authentication paths).

questions.question: In SPHINCS+, the same FORS instance is always used regardless of the message being signed.
questions.explanation: The message and randomizer determine which FORS instance (at which hypertree leaf position) is used. Different messages will typically use different FORS instances, which is what enables stateless operation.
